title: EU and NATO Accession
summary: North Macedonia joined NATO in 2020 and opened EU accession negotiations in 2022, following the Prespa Agreement resolving the name dispute with Greece. Progress in EU negotiations depends on anti-corruption reforms, judicial independence, and — in a new Bulgarian condition — recognition of historical and linguistic connections.
detail:
North Macedonia's path to Euro-Atlantic integration required resolving two simultaneous diplomatic disputes. The first—the name dispute with Greece—was resolved by the 2018 Prespa Agreement, under which the country renamed itself from the Former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia to North Macedonia; in return Greece lifted its veto on NATO and EU membership. The country joined NATO in 2020.

The second dispute—with Bulgaria over historical and linguistic identity questions—has proven harder to resolve. Bulgaria, which joined the EU in 2007, conditioned its agreement to open EU accession negotiations on North Macedonia amending its constitution to recognise Bulgarians as a constituent people and on a broader bilateral reconciliation framework. This condition was met with deep domestic controversy in North Macedonia, where it was seen as an attack on Macedonian national identity and historical narrative.

North Macedonia opened formal EU accession negotiations in July 2022, alongside Albania. Progress in the negotiations has been modest, partly due to the bilateral Bulgaria condition and partly due to domestic governance challenges. The European Commission continues to identify rule of law, judicial independence, and anti-corruption as priority areas.

For the Macedonian diaspora—concentrated in Australia, Switzerland, Germany, and the US—NATO and EU membership represent normalcy and security guarantees that previous generations lacked. The identity concessions required by the Prespa and Sofia processes generate ambivalence: valued outcomes but difficult means.

title: Bulgarian Veto and Minority Rights
summary: Bulgaria has conditioned North Macedonia's EU accession progress on changes to the constitution to recognise Bulgarians as a constituent people and on a broader bilateral framework for historical and linguistic reconciliation. The issue has generated significant domestic controversy and is a central obstacle in the accession process.
detail:
Bulgaria's conditions for North Macedonia's EU accession progress represent a contested use of EU enlargement as leverage for bilateral historical and identity disputes. Bulgaria argues that North Macedonia systematically denies historical and ethnic connections between the two countries' populations and has built a national identity partly on anti-Bulgarian historical narratives. Constitutional recognition of Bulgarians as a constituent people, alongside other changes, was requested as a precondition.

The Bulgarian constitutional amendment enabling recognition was adopted in 2024 after a prolonged political crisis in North Macedonia, where the government faced accusations of capitulating to Bulgarian pressure. The amendment was deeply unpopular domestically and required difficult coalition management. Its ratification set a precedent for external preconditions being imposed on a candidate country's constitution as a condition of accession progress.

Minority rights more broadly—including the rights of Albanians (approximately 25% of the population), Turks, Roma, Serbs, and other communities—are both EU accession requirements and domestic political issues. The Ohrid Framework Agreement of 2001, which ended an armed insurgency by ethnic Albanian groups, established power-sharing arrangements and linguistic rights that continue to shape Macedonian governance.

For human rights organisations and EU-level commentators, the Bulgarian veto episode raised fundamental questions about whether EU enlargement conditionality should be allowed to be exploited for bilateral political purposes unrelated to EU membership criteria. The precedent set in the North Macedonia case has implications for future enlargement rounds.

title: Political Polarisation and Corruption
summary: North Macedonia has experienced sharp political polarisation, including a 2017 political crisis involving allegations of mass wiretapping by the then-government. Anti-corruption reform, public prosecutor independence, and the rule of law remain ongoing EU accession requirements and domestic political battlegrounds.
detail:
North Macedonia's political history since independence has been marked by sharp polarisation between the VMRO-DPMNE and SDSM parties, punctuated by a serious political crisis in 2017 when recordings released by SDSM leader Zoran Zaev (the 'bombs') revealed massive illegal wiretapping by the then-VMRO-led government of over 20,000 people, including politicians, journalists, diplomats, and civil servants.

The crisis triggered mass protests, EU and US mediation, an interim government, and eventually elections that brought SDSM to power. Multiple prosecutions followed under the Special Prosecutor's Office established to handle the wiretapping cases; the political battles over those prosecutions, the independence of the SPO, and the eventual handing of cases to regular prosecutors have continued.

Anti-corruption reform and the independence of the prosecution remain central EU accession benchmarks. The European Commission's progress reports note an improved legal framework but persistent implementation challenges: high-level corruption cases moving slowly through courts, judicial pressures, and whistleblower protection inadequacies.

For North Macedonian civil society, the wiretapping affair was a watershed that exposed the depth of institutional capture and mobilised a civic movement. That movement's energy has partially sustained, though political disillusionment has also grown as reform progress has been slower than hoped. The country's experience has been studied as a case of both authoritarian backsliding and subsequent democratic recovery.

title: EU Integration and Dayton Constitutional Reform
summary: Bosnia and Herzegovina received EU candidate status in 2022. However, the Dayton Peace Agreement's constitutional structure — with its ethnic power-sharing requirements — is widely considered an obstacle to both efficient governance and EU accession requirements on non-discrimination. Reform of the constitution is politically contentious.
detail:
Bosnia and Herzegovina received EU candidate status in December 2022, but the path to opening formal accession negotiations remains blocked by fundamental constitutional obstacles. The 1995 Dayton Peace Agreement—which ended the war—enshrined ethnic power-sharing between Bosniaks, Serbs, and Croats as the foundation of the constitutional order. The EU's non-discrimination requirements conflict directly with some Dayton provisions that exclude citizens not belonging to one of the three constituent peoples from running for certain offices.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled, in Sejdić and Finci v. Bosnia (2009) and subsequent cases, that the constitutional exclusion of Roma, Jews, and others from certain offices violates the ECHR. Bosnia has not implemented these judgments. Constitutional reform would require agreement among the ethnically-defined political blocs, each of which uses the existing system to protect its community's guaranteed positions.

EU accession reform has proceeded unevenly across the fourteen conditions the Commission identified. Some legislative alignment has occurred; others—particularly reforms requiring constitutional change—have barely begun. The political system's design creates incentives for each ethnic bloc to obstruct reforms that might reduce their guaranteed power, making the collective action problem severe.

For Bosnia's internationally dispersed diaspora—Bosniak communities in Germany, Austria, Sweden, the US, and elsewhere, as well as Bosnian Croats in Croatia and Germany and Bosnian Serbs in Serbia and Austria—EU integration offers both practical benefits and normative significance as evidence that the war's wounds are healing.

title: Republika Srpska and Secessionist Tensions
summary: The Republika Srpska entity's political leadership under Milorad Dodik has pursued a strategy of undermining state institutions, denying the Srebrenica genocide, and maintaining close ties with Russia and Serbia. These actions have created significant constitutional crises and drawn EU and US sanctions against Dodik personally.
detail:
Milorad Dodik, President of Republika Srpska (RS), has systematically pursued a strategy of undermining Bosnian state institutions, denying the constitutional authority of the High Representative, disputing the legally established facts about the Srebrenica genocide, and maintaining economic and political ties with Russia that diverge from Bosnia's official EU integration path.

Dodik's actions have included passing RS-level laws that contradict state-level legislation, refusing to implement Constitutional Court decisions, and publicly advocating for RS secession from Bosnia—actions that the international community considers violations of the Dayton Agreement. The US and EU imposed personal sanctions on Dodik, and the High Representative has used Bonn Powers to impose legislation that RS has refused to implement.

The genocide denial issue has been particularly sensitive: the International Court of Justice, the ICTY, and the Bosnian Constitutional Court have all affirmed that the Srebrenica massacre of 1995 constitutes genocide. Dodik and RS institutions have denied or minimised this characterisation, and RS authorities have refused to mark Srebrenica commemoration days established by Bosnian state law.

Russia's support for Dodik—through diplomatic protection at the UN Security Council, financial relationships, and political endorsement—has made the RS challenge to Bosnian constitutional order inseparable from the broader geopolitical contest over the Western Balkans' orientation. For Bosniak civil society and diaspora, the Dodik challenge represents an ongoing existential question about whether the post-war settlement can be maintained.

title: Migration Pressure and Balkan Route
summary: Bosnia has been a significant transit country for irregular migrants using the Western Balkan route to reach the EU. The management of reception facilities, conditions for migrants and asylum seekers, and cooperation with EU border management have been recurring domestic and diplomatic issues.
detail:
Bosnia and Herzegovina has been one of the most challenging Western Balkan states in which to manage irregular migration flows along the Balkan route to the EU. Migrants and asylum seekers transiting from the Western Mediterranean route or through Serbia use Bosnia as a staging point before attempting to cross into Croatia (now in Schengen). The management of this transit population—in camps, informal settlements, and border areas—has strained state resources and generated domestic political tension.

The Una-Sana canton in northwest Bosnia has been the primary concentration point, with informal camps in the Lipa area experiencing severe conditions including fires and overcrowding. International organisations including IOM, UNHCR, and Danish Refugee Council have provided assistance, but resource constraints and political disagreements between federal and entity governments have complicated consistent management.

Croatia's alleged pushbacks from the Schengen external border have left some migrants stranded in Bosnia for extended periods, unable to proceed and unable to access protection. Bosnia has neither the legal framework nor the institutional capacity for comprehensive asylum processing, and the EU has provided financial support for reception management while pressing for institutional strengthening.

For Bosnia's EU accession process, migration management is listed among the reform requirements in Chapter 24 (justice, freedom, and security). The practical challenge is significant: Bosnia must build systems that meet EU standards while managing an active transit flow with limited resources and fragmented governance.

title: EU Accession and Anti-Corruption Reform
summary: Montenegro has been in EU accession negotiations since 2012 and has opened all 33 chapters. Progress has been blocked by slow implementation of anti-corruption commitments and judicial reform. The EU has signalled that negotiations will not be concluded without credible track records on rule of law.
detail:
Montenegro has been in EU accession negotiations longer than any other candidate country, having opened talks in 2012 and completed all 33 chapters. Progress has stalled primarily because the most critical chapters—23 (judiciary, fundamental rights) and 24 (justice, freedom, security)—remain unfinished due to insufficient judicial reform and anti-corruption track record. The EU has explicitly stated that negotiations will not conclude without credible results in these areas.

The judicial system in Montenegro has been characterised by a culture of impunity for high-level corruption, with prosecutions of prominent political and business figures rarely resulting in convictions proportionate to the allegations. The High Judicial Council's independence has been questioned, and the Special State Prosecutor's anti-corruption record has been a particular focus of EU criticism.

Montenegro's political landscape has become more volatile since 2020, when a protest movement linked to church property disputes brought a coalition of opposition groups to power—the first change of government since independence in 2006. Subsequent governments have been short-lived and fragmented, with coalitions shifting unpredictably and the reform mandate disputed between competing political factions.

For Montenegrin diaspora—significant communities in Serbia, Germany, Switzerland, and the US—EU accession offers the tangible benefit of free movement that would facilitate the cross-border lives many already lead informally. Some diaspora Montenegrins hold Serbian citizenship, which provides EU-adjacent status through Serbia's accession process.

title: Church Property Dispute and Identity Politics
summary: A 2019 law on religious communities triggered a major political and social crisis over the ownership of Serbian Orthodox Church properties in Montenegro, which became embroiled with questions of Montenegrin national identity and the extent of Serbian influence in the country. The political fallout led to a change in government.
detail:
Montenegro's church property law of 2019—requiring religious communities to prove ownership of property before 1918 or cede it to the state—was designed primarily to affect the Serbian Orthodox Church (SOC), which holds many of Montenegro's historically significant religious sites. The law was framed by its proponents as an assertion of Montenegrin national sovereignty and identity; by opponents as an attack on religious freedom and Montenegrin Serbs' cultural rights.

The controversy generated massive street protests organised by the SOC and Serb-oriented political and civic groups, with processions of tens of thousands marching through Montenegrin cities in a demonstration of the church's social mobilisation capacity. The protests ultimately contributed to the coalition that unseated the long-governing DPS in August 2020 elections—the first change of government since independence.

The law was subsequently suspended and a new agreement negotiated between the government and the SOC, avoiding immediate property disputes while establishing a framework for resolving them. The new government, whose coalition includes parties sympathetic to the SOC, has managed the issue more carefully, but the underlying tension about the relationship between the Montenegrin state, Montenegrin identity, and Serbian cultural and religious ties has not been resolved.

The episode illustrates the deep entanglement of national identity, religious affiliation, and political loyalty in Montenegro—a country where the question of 'Montenegrin versus Serb' identity is genuinely contested and politically consequential, and where the Serbian Orthodox Church serves as a focal point for those who feel a stronger affiliation with Serbia than with an independent Montenegrin state.

title: Organised Crime and Political Connections
summary: Montenegro has a disproportionate share of drug trafficking networks relative to its size, with alleged connections between organised crime, the legal economy, and political figures. Prosecution of high-profile organised crime cases, witness protection, and police reform are ongoing EU accession benchmarks.
detail:
Montenegro has a documented problem with organised crime disproportionate to its small size, with drug trafficking networks—particularly cocaine smuggling from South America via Montenegro's Adriatic ports—having significant influence in the local economy and political life. Court proceedings and investigative journalism have documented connections between criminal networks, the legal economy (construction, real estate, hospitality), and political figures across party lines.

The assassination of investigative journalist Duško Jovanović in 2004 and the murder of drug clan figures in inter-group conflicts—including high-profile killings in Dubai, Greece, and domestically—have brought international attention to Montenegro's crime problem. Witness protection has been a persistent weakness in prosecuting organised crime: witnesses face intimidation and violence, and the state's ability to protect them has been insufficient.

The EU accession benchmarks on organised crime require Montenegro to demonstrate sustained prosecution results at the highest levels of criminal networks, improved police-prosecutor coordination, and credible witness protection. Progress in meeting these benchmarks has been cited by the Commission as insufficient, contributing to the overall accession stall.

For the business community and for Montenegro's tourism sector—which drives a growing share of the economy—organised crime connections to real estate and hospitality create due diligence concerns for legitimate international investors and undermine the transparency that EU market integration requires. The country's appeal as a luxury Mediterranean destination depends partly on addressing these underlying governance concerns.

title: International Recognition and UN Membership
summary: Kosovo declared independence in 2008 and is recognised by over 100 states, including the US and most EU members. However, Serbia, Russia, China, and five EU member states do not recognise it, blocking UN membership and creating ongoing legal ambiguity in international forums.
detail:
Kosovo's declaration of independence on 17 February 2008, following nine years of UN administration under Security Council Resolution 1244, is one of the most contested acts of self-determination in post-Cold War international law. The International Court of Justice issued an advisory opinion in 2010 holding that the declaration did not violate international law—a significant but non-binding affirmation that did not resolve the underlying political dispute.

The non-recognition by Serbia, Russia, China, and five EU member states (Spain, Slovakia, Romania, Greece, Cyprus—each with their own sensitivities about self-determination) has prevented Kosovo from joining the United Nations and from membership in international organisations including Interpol and UNESCO, creating practical and symbolic disadvantages. Kosovo's quasi-international status is managed through bilateral recognition, Schengen visa access (from January 2024), and membership in regional organisations including the Council of Europe (admitted in 2024).

Full international recognition and UN membership require either a Security Council decision (vetoed by Russia) or a different diplomatic pathway. Diplomatic efforts have focused on normalising relations with Serbia as a de facto path to broader recognition, though progress in the EU-mediated dialogue has been limited.

For the Kosovo diaspora—very large relative to the domestic population, with major communities in Germany, Switzerland, the UK, the US, and across Western Europe—the recognition question has both symbolic and practical dimensions. Diaspora Kosovars are significant economic contributors through remittances and investment, and their political engagement sustains the international recognition project.

title: EU Visa Liberalisation
summary: Kosovo became the last Western Balkan jurisdiction to receive EU visa-free travel, in January 2024. The change has significant economic and social implications for Kosovo's large diaspora and for freedom of movement. EU integration — including the official opening of accession negotiations — remains a central political objective.
detail:
Kosovo became the last Western Balkan territory to receive EU Schengen visa-free access, with the decision entering into force in January 2024 after years of delay. The liberalisation followed Kosovo meeting all the required benchmarks on border management, travel documents, anti-corruption measures, and irregular migration control. The delay relative to other Western Balkan states (Serbia and North Macedonia received liberalisation in 2009) created a sense of discrimination that was deeply felt.

The practical impact has been significant: Kosovars can now travel freely to EU Schengen states for short visits, facilitating family reunification visits, business, study exploration, and cultural exchange. For a country where remittances from the diaspora constitute approximately 15-20% of GDP, easier physical connection between diaspora and family members has direct economic and social consequences.

Kosovo's formal EU accession process has not yet opened—the five non-recognising EU member states create a diplomatic complication, and the general accession framework requires unanimous Council agreement. Kosovo has applied for EU membership and the Commission has assessed it as a potential candidate, but the political path to opening negotiations is unclear.

For the Kosovo diaspora—with over 700,000 Kosovars estimated in Germany alone, and significant communities in Switzerland, Austria, Sweden, and the US—visa liberalisation is a major quality-of-life improvement but also a reminder of the incomplete international status that still affects Kosovo's integration into international structures.

title: North Kosovo Tensions and Serb Community
summary: The Serb-majority municipalities of northern Kosovo have been a persistent source of tension, including armed incidents, boycotts of Kosovo institutions, and the presence of armed Serbian structures. EU-mediated dialogue between Pristina and Belgrade has produced agreements that remain largely unimplemented.
detail:
The four northern Kosovo municipalities—Leposavić, Zvečan, Zubin Potok, and Mitrovica North—where ethnic Serbs are the majority, have been a persistent flashpoint in Kosovo-Serbia relations. The Serb community there has maintained strong ties with Belgrade, boycotted Kosovo institutions, and been supported by parallel Serbian state structures including healthcare, education, and security elements operating without Kosovo government authorisation.

Major incidents have included barricades erected across roads into the north in 2022, armed clashes near the Banjska village in September 2023, and sustained tensions over the removal of Serbian license plates and the designation of the Kosovo dinar as the sole legal tender. KFOR (NATO Kosovo Force) has played a crucial stabilisation role, maintaining roads open and preventing escalation, though its position has been tested by the scale of the challenges.

EU-mediated dialogue on normalisation has produced agreements—most significantly the obligation on Serbia to allow Kosovo's participation in regional organisations—that have been incompletely implemented. The Association of Serb-majority Municipalities, agreed in 2013, has never been established by Kosovo, creating a justification for Serbian non-performance on other commitments.

For Kosovo's Serb community, the situation creates a genuine dilemma: integration into Kosovo institutions would mean accepting Kosovo's sovereignty, which many reject; but the current limbo leaves them without some state services and without clear prospects. Some have emigrated to Serbia; others maintain their current status hoping for a political resolution.

title: Political Prisoners and 2020 Election Aftermath
summary: Following the disputed 2020 presidential election and mass protests, Belarusian authorities detained over 35,000 people and sentenced hundreds of political prisoners. The UN Special Rapporteur on Belarus and organisations including Viasna Human Rights Center continue to document ongoing repression and the situation of those imprisoned.

title: Complicity in Russia's War in Ukraine
summary: Belarus permitted Russia to use its territory as a launch point for the February 2022 invasion of Ukraine and has continued to provide logistical support. The EU, US, and UK have imposed successive rounds of sanctions on Belarus. The country's role in the war and the accountability of the Lukashenko government are subjects of international legal and political debate.

title: Exile Opposition and Democratic Aspirations
summary: The Coordination Council of the Belarusian democratic opposition, led abroad by Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya, claims to represent the democratic majority that it argues won the 2020 election. The exile government maintains diplomatic recognition from several states and advocates for targeted sanctions, support for political prisoners, and a democratic transition.

title: Political Prisoners and Navalny's Legacy
summary: Alexei Navalny died in an Arctic penal colony in February 2024. His Anti-Corruption Foundation (FBK) continues to operate in exile, publishing investigations into official corruption. Thousands of Russians have been imprisoned for political activity, anti-war speech, or journalism. OVD-Info tracks these cases and provides legal support.

title: Media Freedom and the Information Environment
summary: Russia has effectively eliminated independent domestic media. Outlets including Novaya Gazeta, Echo of Moscow, and Dozhd TV were shut down or forced into exile following the 2022 invasion of Ukraine. Meduza, The Insider, and iStories continue to operate from abroad. Access to independent information inside Russia relies on VPNs and anti-censorship tools.

title: War in Ukraine and International Accountability
summary: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, launched in February 2022, is the subject of multiple international accountability processes, including the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant for President Vladimir Putin for the deportation of Ukrainian children. War crimes documentation by Ukrainian prosecutors, international NGOs, and the ICC shapes debates about future accountability.

title: Human Rights Conditions and UN Documentation
summary: The UN Commission of Inquiry on Human Rights in North Korea (2014) found that systematic, widespread and gross human rights violations amount to crimes against humanity. Conditions including political prison camps (kwanliso), public executions, enforced disappearances, and severe restrictions on movement and information are documented by the UN and NGOs.

title: Nuclear Programme and International Sanctions
summary: North Korea has conducted six nuclear tests and developed intercontinental ballistic missiles capable of reaching the continental United States. UN Security Council sanctions prohibit arms exports, limit energy imports, and restrict financial flows. The sanctions regime's enforcement — particularly by China and Russia — and the prospects for denuclearisation diplomacy are ongoing international policy questions.

title: Defector Support and Exile Community
summary: An estimated 33,000 North Koreans have settled in South Korea; smaller numbers live in the UK, US, Europe, and Southeast Asia. Organizations including Liberty in North Korea (LiNK), the North Korea Database Center for Human Rights (NKDB), and the Citizens' Alliance for North Korean Human Rights document conditions and support defector resettlement and advocacy.

title: Woman, Life, Freedom Movement
summary: The death of Mahsa Amini in morality police custody in September 2022 triggered the largest sustained protest movement in Iran's post-revolutionary history. The government's crackdown — including over 500 deaths, mass arrests, and executions of protesters — drew international condemnation. The movement's demands for women's rights and systemic political change remain active.

title: Nuclear Programme and International Diplomacy
summary: Iran's nuclear programme — including uranium enrichment at levels close to weapons-grade — has been the subject of repeated international negotiations. The 2015 JCPOA was abandoned by the US in 2018; subsequent talks have not produced a renewed agreement. Escalation risks, IAEA monitoring access, and the diplomatic path forward are ongoing concerns.

title: Internet Shutdowns and Digital Rights
summary: Iran operates one of the world's most restrictive internet environments, including the National Information Network (SHOMA) designed to enable disconnection from the global internet. During protests, authorities have repeatedly shut down mobile internet and VPN access. Digital rights groups document shutdowns, platform blocks, and surveillance of activists.

title: July 2021 Protests and Political Repression
summary: The July 11, 2021 protests — the largest in Cuba since 1959 — were triggered by economic shortages, power cuts, and demands for political change. The government's response included mass arrests, trials of protesters in military courts, and sentences of up to 25 years. Hundreds remain imprisoned.

title: Economic Crisis and Mass Emigration
summary: Cuba faces its worst economic crisis in decades, marked by fuel shortages, food insecurity, hyperinflation of the peso, and electricity blackouts lasting up to 20 hours daily. Emigration has accelerated dramatically, with over 500,000 Cubans estimated to have left for the United States alone in 2022–2023, representing a historic demographic exodus.

title: Internet Access and Digital Civil Society
summary: Cuba significantly expanded mobile internet access in 2018, enabling a new generation of digital civil society. The government has also used connectivity to mobilise and surveil. Restricted access during protest periods, blocks on social media platforms, and harassment of digital journalists are documented by digital rights organizations.

title: 2024 Presidential Election Dispute
summary: The July 2024 presidential election in Venezuela produced a disputed outcome. The Maduro government claimed victory but declined to release precinct-level results; opposition monitors tabulated results showing a decisive win for candidate Edmundo González. The Carter Center and other observers found the process lacked transparency. González went into exile in September 2024.

title: Migration Crisis and Diaspora
summary: Over 7.7 million Venezuelans — approximately a quarter of the pre-crisis population — have left the country since 2015, creating the largest displacement crisis in Latin America. Major host countries include Colombia, Peru, Ecuador, Brazil, Chile, and the United States. The humanitarian, economic, and political dimensions of this diaspora are a major regional policy challenge.

title: Humanitarian Situation and Sanctions
summary: Venezuela's humanitarian crisis — marked by poverty, malnutrition, collapsing healthcare, and chronic shortages — has been shaped by both government mismanagement and international sanctions. Debates about the effectiveness and humanitarian impact of US and EU sanctions, and the conditions for their lifting, are central to international policy discussions on Venezuela.

title: Hong Kong National Security Law and Civil Liberties
summary: China imposed the National Security Law on Hong Kong in June 2020, criminalising acts of secession, subversion, terrorism, and collusion with foreign forces. Over 260 people have been prosecuted under the law, including members of the pro-democracy opposition and journalists. The 2021 restructuring of the Legislative Council eliminated most directly elected seats.

title: Press Freedom and Media Control
summary: China operates one of the most restrictive media environments in the world. Major independent Hong Kong outlets including Apple Daily and Stand News were shut down following NSL prosecutions. On the mainland, the Great Firewall blocks major international platforms and foreign news sites. The Committee to Protect Journalists and Reporters Without Borders document journalist detentions and outlet closures.

title: EU-China Trade Relations and Decoupling Debate
summary: EU-China trade relations are subject to growing friction, including EU anti-subsidy investigations into Chinese electric vehicles and solar panels, restrictions on technology transfers, and debates about strategic dependencies. The concept of 'de-risking' — reducing exposure to Chinese supply chains in critical sectors — is shaping EU industrial and trade policy.
